name: Thomas Cook
summary: Britain's oldest travel agent collapsed into compulsory liquidation in 2019 under £1.7B of debt, triggering the largest peacetime repatriation in UK history.
lesson: Legacy travel giants weighed down by debt and physical infrastructure can be wiped out fast by shifting booking habits.

name: WOW air
summary: Icelandic ultra-low-cost long-haul carrier grew rapidly on cheap transatlantic fares but ran out of cash and ceased operations.
lesson: Ultra-low fares built on unsustainable unit economics collapse the moment fuel costs or funding tighten.

name: Jet Airways
summary: Once India's largest private airline grounded its entire fleet under $1.2B of debt; a 2021 revival attempt also failed.
lesson: Heavily leveraged airline expansion without disciplined cost control eventually catches up with even market leaders.

name: Spirit Airlines
summary: US ultra-low-cost carrier filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after a proposed JetBlue merger was blocked by regulators and it couldn't return to profitability post-COVID.
lesson: A blocked exit can leave a structurally unprofitable business with no path forward but bankruptcy.

name: Sonder Holdings
summary: Tech-enabled short-term rental operator went public via SPAC, saw a proposed Marriott partnership collapse, and wound down operations in 2025.
lesson: Leasing large real-estate portfolios to run a hospitality brand requires far deeper capital reserves than software-style startups assume.

name: Vacasa
summary: Largest US vacation rental property manager went public via SPAC, lost over 95% of its stock value amid heavy losses, and was taken private by Casago.
lesson: Rapid roll-up growth in property management can mask deteriorating per-unit profitability until public markets expose it.

name: Selina
summary: Latin America-born hostel/co-living hospitality brand expanded to 150+ locations, went public via SPAC, and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy amid unsustainable lease obligations.
lesson: Rapid global real-estate expansion without proving profitability at existing locations first is a recurring hospitality failure pattern.

name: Fyre Media (Fyre Festival)
summary: Luxury music festival venture collapsed spectacularly when the promised Bahamas festival turned out to be built on fraudulent marketing, leading to the founder's imprisonment.
lesson: Marketing hype divorced from operational reality in event/travel experiences can constitute outright fraud, not just failure.

name: Getaround
summary: Peer-to-peer car-sharing marketplace went public via SPAC, then faced severe financial distress, delisting from the NYSE, and restructuring.
lesson: SPAC mergers exposed several travel-adjacent marketplaces' weak unit economics to public markets faster than they could fix them.

name: Karhoo
summary: Ride-hailing marketplace aggregating taxis and airport transfers raised huge funding but collapsed amid a board dispute and cash mismanagement.
lesson: Massive funding rounds can't compensate for a dysfunctional board and leadership team burning cash without a coherent strategy.

name: Airbnb
summary: Home-sharing marketplace grew from air-mattress rentals into the world's largest hospitality platform, surviving a COVID-19 near-death experience to go public in 2020.
lesson: Deep two-sided network effects and brand trust let a marketplace recover even from a near-total demand collapse.

name: Booking.com
summary: Dutch hotel booking site grew, after being acquired by Priceline, into the world's largest online travel agency by gross bookings.
lesson: Disciplined, metrics-driven performance marketing can out-execute flashier competitors over the long run.

name: Expedia Group
summary: Online travel agency spun out of Microsoft grew into one of the world's largest travel booking companies spanning multiple brands.
lesson: Building a multi-brand portfolio strategy let one OTA capture different customer segments simultaneously.

name: Skyscanner
summary: Scottish flight comparison and metasearch site grew into a leading global travel search engine, acquired by China's Trip.com Group in 2016 for roughly $1.75B.
lesson: Independent metasearch tools can become highly valuable acquisition targets for OTAs seeking global distribution.

name: HotelTonight
summary: Last-minute, mobile-first hotel booking app built a loyal user base and was acquired by Airbnb in 2019 for roughly $400M.
lesson: A sharply focused niche can build enough brand loyalty to earn a premium acquisition.

name: GetYourGuide
summary: Berlin-based tours and activities booking platform grew into a global unicorn connecting travelers with local experience providers worldwide.
lesson: Aggregating fragmented local tour operators into one reliable booking platform created durable two-sided network effects.

name: Hopper
summary: Mobile-first flight and hotel booking app known for price prediction and 'fintech for travel' add-ons relaunched under the Hopper name in 2015 and grew into a profitable travel unicorn.
lesson: Layering fintech-style products onto travel booking created new high-margin revenue beyond commissions.

name: Turo
summary: Peer-to-peer car-sharing marketplace grew into the leading US car-sharing platform and filed to go public.
lesson: A true peer-to-peer marketplace model kept Turo's capital intensity far lower than fleet-owning rivals.

name: Mews
summary: Cloud-based hotel property management system grew into a unicorn, replacing legacy on-premise hotel software.
lesson: Modernizing decades-old legacy hotel software created a large greenfield opportunity for a cloud-native challenger.

name: FlixBus
summary: German intercity bus network built on an asset-light franchise model grew into a pan-European and later US unicorn.
lesson: An asset-light network model let FlixBus scale transportation infrastructure far faster and cheaper than owning a fleet.
